hook: 1950s mail-sorting depot saved from the wrecking ball, now three floors of art
facts: Built in the 1950s to sort the city's post, between the two main stations
Slated for demolition until Lille 2004 (European Capital of Culture) reprieved it
Three exhibition levels totalling about 6,000 square metres
Programmed by lille3000 for biennale shows, gigs and parties
w: For decades it swallowed the city's letters by the sackful, a grey sorting shed between the two stations. Marked for the bulldozer, then rescued when Lille became Capital of Culture in 2004. Three floors of mail chutes now hang biennale art and throw all-night parties. Post repurposed.

hook: Bronze goddess clutching a cannon fuse, raised to a siege that refused to surrender
facts: Inaugurated 1845 on the Grand'Place (now Place du General-de-Gaulle)
Commemorates the 1792 Austrian siege the city withstood
Statue by Theophile Bra; column and base by architect Charles Benvignat
She points to the mayor's recorded refusal to capitulate
w: In 1792 the Austrians shelled the town for a week and the people refused to fold. Half a century on the city crowned its square with this bronze woman gripping a cannon fuse, her free hand pointing at the mayor's recorded 'no' to surrender. Locals nicknamed her the Goddess. Defiance, cast in metal.

hook: Listed 18th-century brewery turned cross-disciplinary arts factory
facts: Set in the former Corman-Vandame / Trois Moulins brewery, brewing ended 1934
Brewery buildings listed as Monument historique
Reopened March 2004 for Lille European Capital of Culture
Runs concerts, residencies and exhibitions across two courtyards
w: Beer flowed here until 1934, when the Corman-Vandame works fell quiet. The brick brewery, now protected, reopened in 2004 as one of the city's Maisons Folies: concerts in the malt-house, artists camped in residency, exhibitions strung across two courtyards. Hops out, ideas in. Industry, brewed anew.

hook: Domed rotunda church, deconsecrated, Lille's only round nave
facts: Rebuilt inside Vauban's ramparts from 1675, opened for worship 1677
Rare circular plan under a dome rising about 55 metres
Damaged in the 1708 siege; dome rebuilt by 1713
Deconsecrated since 1989 and used for exhibitions
w: When Vauban's ramparts swallowed the old parish, the city built this oddity inside a bastion: a round church under a dome, the only circular nave in town. Battered in the 1708 siege, patched up by 1713, emptied of prayer since 1989. Locals call it the Big Madeleine. Now a hall for art, lit from above.

n: Beffroi de la Chambre de Commerce
hook: 76-metre neo-Flemish belfry whose carillon plays 'P'tit Quinquin' on the quarter-hour
facts: Built 1910-1921 to designs by Louis-Marie Cordonnier, work halted by WWI
Belfry rises about 76 metres over the Opera
Carillon of 25 bells installed by the Friends of the Carillons of Lille
Called the Nouvelle Bourse, facing the Vieille Bourse
w: Cordonnier started this neo-Flemish tower in 1910; the war stopped him, and it was finished only in 1921. Seventy-six metres of brick over the Opera, topped by 25 bells that the locals paid for by subscription. Every quarter-hour they ring out the lullaby 'P'tit Quinquin'. Commerce, with a tune.

hook: Cult contemporary-music venue tucked on a walkway inside a shopping mall
facts: Running since 1989, in Euralille since 1995
Two rooms on the upper walkway of the Westfield Euralille centre
Programme spans rock, electro, metal, hip hop and reggae
Holds the French Ministry of Culture SMAC label
w: Few cities bury their best gig room inside a mall, but Lille does: up a walkway above the Euralille shops, past the food court, two sweat-box rooms that have booked the cutting edge since 1989. Rock to electro to metal, state-backed but feral. The unlikeliest address in town. Loud above the tills.

hook: 1680s row of fourteen matched houses still pocked with siege cannonballs
facts: Built 1685-1687 by architect Julien Destree, mason Simon Vollant
Fourteen uniform facades blending French classical and Flemish detail
Cherub-head pilasters and fruit-garland carving
Cannonballs from the 1792 siege embedded in the right-hand facade
w: After Louis XIV took the town, a single architect rebuilt this whole side of the square in one go: fourteen houses with one face, cherub pilasters and carved fruit, French order softened by Flemish swagger. Look up the right end and count the cannonballs lodged in the brick since 1792. Damage, kept.

hook: Flamboyant Gothic fragment of the Burgundian dukes' lost palace, now the tourist office
facts: Begun 1453 for Philip the Good, Duke of Burgundy; completed 1473
Once four wings around a courtyard; mostly lost to fires, one in 1916
Surviving Conclave hall, 15th-century stair and guardroom remain
Houses the city tourist office today
w: Philip the Good raised a four-winged Gothic palace here in the 1450s, the Burgundian court at full glittering tilt. Fire ate most of it, the last blaze in 1916, leaving only the Conclave hall, a spiral stair and the guardroom. Dukes once feasted where you now pick up a city map. Splendour, in fragments.

hook: 104-metre concrete belfry guarded by the two giants of the city's founding legend
facts: Town hall built 1924-1932 by Emile Dubuisson in reinforced concrete
At 104 metres, Europe's tallest civic belfry
Giants Lyderic and Phinaert carved straight into wet concrete by Carlo Sarrabezolles
Inscribed on UNESCO's belfries list in 2005
legend: Legend says the knight Lyderic slew the giant brigand Phinaert and founded Lille
w: Dubuisson poured this 104-metre tower in raw concrete between the wars, the tallest civic belfry in Europe, and a sculptor carved its two giants straight into the wet stone. Legend says the knight Lyderic killed the brigand giant Phinaert here and founded the town. Now they guard the lift to the view.
